posts.title: How to keep blonde hair bright between appointments
posts.excerpt: A simple care rhythm for brightness, hydration, and tone without overusing purple shampoo.
body: Healthy blonde hair is less about one miracle product and more about rhythm. Use a gentle shampoo, rotate in hydration, and save purple shampoo for when the tone actually looks warm.
Heat protection matters every time. Even a quick pass with a hot tool can make lightened hair feel dull if it is unprotected.
Book gloss appointments before the color feels tired. A toner refresh can bring back softness and shine without a full blonding session.

posts.title: What to expect at a color correction consultation
posts.excerpt: A transparent look at strand tests, realistic timelines, and why correction work starts with a plan.
body: A correction consultation begins with history: previous color, at-home products, heat habits, and the look you want next.
The safest plan is usually staged. That protects the hair and gives each appointment a clear target instead of chasing a result in one risky sitting.
Photos help, but daylight photos of your current hair help even more. They give your stylist a better read on tone, banding, and condition.

posts.title: The difference between gloss, toner, and glaze
posts.excerpt: Three common salon words, explained in plain language so you can book the right refresh.
body: A toner adjusts color direction after lightening. It can soften warmth, make blonde creamier, or shift brunette into a cooler or richer family.
A gloss focuses on shine and tone. Many glosses add a sheer veil of color while making the hair feel polished.
A glaze is often used as a soft shine service. Product lines use the words differently, so the best choice depends on your current hair and your goal.
